medications.indication_rationale: Extensive multi-site facial impetigo warrants oral anti-staphylococcal/streptococcal antibiotics; source used flucloxacillin 500mg four times daily for 7 days.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 500mg orally four times daily for 7 days (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: First-line for LOCALISED impetigo after crust removal; here disease is extensive so oral therapy is preferred, though topical may be adjunctive.
The OTC hydrocortisone must be STOPPED — it suppresses local immunity and accelerates impetigo spread.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Soften and gently remove crusts with warm soapy water before applying treatment; stop the steroid cream; wash hands after wound care.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Crust removal lets antibiotic reach bacteria; the steroid worsens spread.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Contagion control: contagious until 24h of antibiotics/crusts resolved; wash all towels/linen in hot water; don't share personal items; cover lesions.

patient_education_points.point: This is impetigo ('school sores') — a contagious superficial bacterial skin infection. Because it's spread across your face, oral antibiotics are the right treatment; it should stop spreading within about 48 hours and heal in 7–10 days.

Stop the hydrocortisone cream (it makes impetigo worse). Gently wash off crusts, wash your hands, wash towels/linen in hot water, don't share items, and you're contagious until 24 hours of antibiotics.

Go to ED / call 000 if the redness becomes deep, hot and very painful, you get a high fever with shivering, or your eye swells. And see a doctor if your urine turns dark/cola-coloured in the next few weeks (a rare kidney complication).

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Lesions drying and not spreading; adherence; complications.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Deep spreading painful redness, high fever, eye swelling — ED; dark urine in 1–3 weeks — GP.

must_include_items: Recognise impetigo and treat by extent (oral antibiotics for extensive facial disease)
Contagion-control advice (24h contagious, wash linen, no sharing, crust removal)
Cellulitis/sepsis safety-net + post-strep GN (dark urine) safety-net
acceptable_alternatives: Cephalexin as alternative oral antibiotic; topical mupirocin if disease were localised
errors_of_commission: Topical antibiotic alone for extensive facial impetigo
Antivirals (treating as HSV) without HSV features
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires appropriate antibiotic by extent, stopping the steroid, contagion control, and a cellulitis + PSGN safety-net.
